narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 44% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. In reviewing your profile, we see you ranked in the second decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, well below the typical student's fifth or sixth decile position. Your LSAT score of 32.3 also falls short of the average 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 is lower than the usual 3.21. As a Black female from a middle-income family planning to attend part-time, you're in underrepresented groups compared to the dataset's majority of White, full-time male students from varied incomes. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your third-year decile rank, which had the biggest push toward predicting bar failure. Next in influence came your LSAT score, followed closely by your first-year decile rank, both nudging the prediction in the same unfavorable direction. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, part-time status, and gender rounded out the factors, each contributing a bit more toward the failure risk in descending order of impact. Overall, these elements together painted a picture riskier than for students with stronger academic rankings and test scores. While some factors like your background are fixed, you could realistically improve your odds by aiming to boost future academic performance through dedicated study habits or tutoring to climb those decile rankings. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score is another actionable step if you're eligible, as it carries significant weight. Switching to full-time enrollment might also help, given how part-time status slightly tipped the scales against you. With focused preparation tailored to the bar exam, many students overcome similar predictions—consider targeted prep courses or mentorship to build those key strengths. We're rooting for your success, and refining these areas could open doors next time.
